title: The Plan
text:
Efforts to anticipate the consequences of a landslide occurring commonly use three sequential phases of analysis:

Susceptibility. Where landslides occur.
Hazard. How often and how big.
Risk. The consequences - costs - if a landslide occurs.

This study is focused on susceptibility. We examine susceptibility in three ways:

Susceptibility to landslide initiation.
Extent of landslide runout.
Linking susceptibility to initiation with likely runout extent to determine susceptibility to landslides that initiate upslope.

With previous analyses using the Tongass Landslide Inventory, I used a frequency-ratio approach for initiation and survival analysis for runout. I like the frequency-ratio method because it provides a direct measure of landslide density and uses information over the entire study area, rather than subsampling, but I want to compare it to other options. In particular, I want to explore methods that better incorporate the combined effects of multiple covariates for assessing susceptibility to landslide initiation. Topography inferred using a lidar DEM provides an important set of covariates associated with landslide location. For Prince of Wales, I used a topographic index calculated as partial contributing area times slope. I’d like to try some different options here, including a simple factor-of-safety model, which might combine topographic elements in a physically relevant way to render a single-valued measure of landslide propensity. Derivation of a simple model is described in Section FoS. I will try using this Factor of Safety model as a candidate covariate in multivariate analyses of susceptibility to landslide initiation, along with other potentially useful options.
The Tongass inventory consists of polygons delineating landslide scars mapped from aerial photographs. I want to use the entire polygon to extract topographic, soils, and land-cover attributes. I want to see if we can find relationships with initiation-zone size, inundation area, runout-zone width, and runout length with the underlying terrain attributes. A key component for obtaining these attributes is to create a centerline for each polygon consisting of a series of linked nodes. The nodes are initiated at DEM grid points, but the centerline path may be smoothed. Each node can then be assigned attributes of the polygon associated with the node position in the polygon, such as distance from the upslope end(s) and distance to the downslope end(s). Several rasters can be created for each polygon: distance to edge (which gives polygon width for nodes along the centerline), the frequency distribution of elevation, gradient, curvatures, contributing areas, land cover, and anything else of interest associated with the portion of the polygon closest to each node.
An important objective with this project is to provide a series of markdown notebooks that illustrate the methods and provide the code and data for replication of the analyses. This will enable others to check these results, to apply the methods to other sites, and to expand and improve those methods. I rely partly on programs that I have written in Fortran. The source code is licensed under GPLv3 and executable files can be provided (compiled for the Windows operating system). Much of the statistical modeling and image production is done using R.

title: Characterizing Initiation Locations
text:
We seek to relate landslide locations to measurable attributes of the terrain. Available data for characterizing landslide locations on Wrangell Island are provided by the Tongass National Forest landslide inventory. Available data for characterizing terrain attributes include a lidar-derived DEM and the Tongass National Forest datasets on soils, land cover, and history of forest activities. The types of terrain attributes associated with landsliding can be inferred from physical models of slope instability and from the results of other empirical studies of landslide susceptibility. These studies point to a set of key topographic attributes: slope gradient, local curvatures, and upslope contributing area, all of which can be measured directly or inferred from the DEM. Properties of the soil, including depth, porosity, bulk density, hydraulic transmissivity, friction angle, and cohesion, also play key roles. These properties are not measurable with remotely sensed data, but variations in these properties may be inferred from soil-type mapping.


The Tongass NF landslide inventory provides polygons indicating mapped landslide-scar boundaries. These include both the initiation, runout, and deposition zones.

Here we focus solely on the initiation zones. An initiation zone for each polygon is estimated by extracting the upper portion of the polygon extending a distance downslope from the top equal approximately to the width of the polygon. This is done in code chunk (LS_poly?) with program LS_poly.


Code
library(data.table)
library(TerrainWorksUtils)
library(stringr)

# First we need to run LS_poly to build the raster of initiation zones.

DEM <- paste0(dataFolder, "elev_wrangell.flt")
polyFile <- paste0(dataFolder, "LS_polys_noroad.shp")
polyID <- "ID"

gradRadius <- 15.
diameter <- round(2.*gradRadius,0)
gradFile <- paste0(dataFolder, "Grad_", diameter, ".flt")
if (file.exists(paste0(gradFile))) {
  inGrad <- gradFile
  outGrad <- "nofile"
} else {
  inGrad <- "nofile"
  outGrad <- gradFile
}

tanRadius <- 15.0
diameter <- round(2.*tanRadius,0)
tanFile <- paste0(dataFolder, "Tan_", diameter, ".flt")
if (file.exists(tanFile)) {
  inTan <- tanFile
  outTan <- "nofile"
} else {
  inTan <- "nofile"
  outTan <- tanFile
}

profRadius <- 15.0
diameter <- round(2.*profRadius,0)
profFile <- paste0(dataFolder, "Prof_", diameter, ".flt")
if (file.exists(profFile)) {
  inProf <- profFile
  outProf <- "nofile"
} else {
  inProf <- "nofile"
  outProf <- profFile
}

inFoS <- paste0(dataFolder, "FoS_pca72.flt")
outNodes <- paste0(dataFolder, "centerNodes")
outCSV <- paste0(dataFolder, "outpoly")
outInit <- paste0(dataFolder, "init")
scratchDir <- "c:/work/scratch/"
executableDir <- "c:/work/sandbox/landslideutilities/projects/ls_poly/x64/release"
  
if (!file.exists(paste0(dataFolder, outCSV, "_init.csv"))) {
  returnCode <- TerrainWorksUtils::LS_poly(
    DEM,
    polyFile,
    polyID,
    inGrad,
    inTan,
    inProf,
    inFoS,
    outGrad,
    gradRadius,
    outTan,
    tanRadius,
    outProf,
    profRadius,
    outNodes,
    outCSV,
    outInit,
    scratchDir,
    executableDir)

  if (returnCode != 0) { 
    stop("Error in LS_poly, return code: ", returnCode)
  }
}


As shown in Figure 2, these initiation zones span a range of widths from 10 to 120 meters, with a mean of 30 meters.


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

initStats <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(outCSV, "_init.csv")))

boxarea <- ggplot(initStats, aes(y=sqrt(Area_m2))) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(y = "Sqrt(Area)") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.x = element_blank())

cumarea <- ggplot(initStats, aes(x=sqrt(Area_m2))) + 
  stat_ecdf(geom="step") +
  labs(x = "Sqrt(Area)",
       y = "Proportion of Initiation Zones")

boxwidth <- ggplot(initStats, aes(y=Width_m)) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(y = "Width (m)") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.x = element_blank())

cumwidth <- ggplot(initStats, aes(x=Width_m)) + 
  stat_ecdf(geom="step") +
  labs(x = "Width (m)",
       y = "Proportion of Initiation Zones")

pwidth <- (boxarea + cumarea) / (boxwidth + cumwidth)
pwidth <- pwidth + plot_annotation(
  title = "Estimated Width of Initiation Zones" ,
  caption = "Width (m) is estimated as the square root of the initiation-zone area, shown in the upper panels, and the average width of the entire landslide polygon, shown in the lower panels")

pwidth








Figure 2








We can calculate a variety of topographic indices using the lidar DEM. As mentioned above, topographic attributes expected to be associated with landslide initiation zones include slope gradient, curvature, and flow accumulation. Gradient and curvature are scale-dependent, in that the value can vary with the length over which they are measured. These properties of the topography influence patterns and rates of movement for water and colluvium, thus influencing the depth of soil and the depth of soil saturation during rainstorm and snowmelt events. The pertinent length scales over which these patterns influence soil stability are reflected in the size distribution of the initiation zones. I calculated gradient and curvatures over a 30-meter length, equal to the mean of the initiation-zone widths (Figure 2).
The upslope flow-accumulation zone provides a relative measure of the amount of water flowing to the area represented by a DEM cell during rainstorms. Once subsurface flow through the soil reaches steady state, the discharge of water through a DEM cell is the upslope accumulation area times the rainfall intensity. This steady-state condition may never actually be achieved, so it may be informative to look at accumulation zones associated with storms of a fixed duration. These are calculated with program Partial, which I have prerun for a range of storm durations from 6 to 72 hours. This is an approximate solution using Darcy flow velocity for shallow subsurface flow, assuming that saturated water flow through the soil is parallel to the ground surface and ignoring the time for water to infiltrate to the saturated zone.
I also examine predictions of a simple physical model as a potential predictor, using the Factor of Safety FoS calculated with the infinite slope model assuming uniform soil properties. This model is an abstraction of actual physical conditions that ignores many factors pertinent to soil failure, so the modeled values may not provide a direct estimate of how landslide potential varies spatially. It does, however, provide a single-valued function that incorporates both slope gradient and flow accumulation. I’ll examine the FoS calculated using flow accumulation rasters generated for a 6-hour and 72-hour storm.
There are several options for exploring the degree to which different indices are associated with landslide initiation zones. A starting point is to look at the frequency distribution of index values both inside the initiation zones and over the entire study area. The degree to which these differ indicates the degree to which an index can differentiate initiation zones from the area in general. These distributions are plotted below using both bar plots and cumulative distributions.
To calculate these distributions, I masked the study area to include only those areas that fell within the range of gradient, FoS, and curvature values found within the initiation zones. Values outside of these zones have a landslide density of zero, based on this inventory. I want to focus on those areas of the landscape where landslide density is greater than zero.
For areas falling within any increment of an index value, the proportion of that area that is within the intiation zones provides a measure of landslide density for that increment. Program SamplePoints will extract these areas for each index. The following code chunks run SamplePoints and generate a series of plots showing histograms and cumulative frequency distributions of variable values both inside the initiation zones and over the entire study area. The landslide density associated with each binned inverval of variable values is also shown, with a smoothed line fit through the points. Density is calculated as the initiation-zone area divided by the total area within each interval range. The same vertical scale is used for all the density plots so that they can be compared directly.


Code
# Run sample points to build a raster masking zones outside the range of predictor values
# associated with landslide initiation zones. Edit input variables as needed
work <- "c:/work/data/wrangell/"
inRaster <- paste0(dataFolder,"init") # initiation zones, raster created by LS_poly above
areaPerSample <- 10000. # within initiation zones, one point every areaPerSample square meters
buffer_in <- 10. # buffer around sample points inside initiation zones, in meters
buffer_out <- 3. # buffer around sample points outside initiation zones
margin <- 1. # margin around initiation zones to preclude sample points close to the edge, in meters
ratio <- 3000. # ratio of sample points outside initiation zones to those within
nbins <- 100 # number of bins for building histograms

minPatch <- 100000.
inPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "inpoints1000")
outPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "outpoints") # used below for spatstat
outInit <- paste0(dataFolder, "initMod")
outInitPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "initPoints")
table <- paste0(dataFolder, "table")
scratchDir <- "c:/work/scratch"
executableDir <- "c:/work/sandbox/landslideutilities/projects/samplepoints/x64/release"

returnCode <- TerrainWorksUtils::samplePoints(
  inRaster,
  areaPerSample,
  buffer_in,
  buffer_out,
  margin,
  ratio,
  nbins,
  R4rasters,
  I4rasters,
  minPatch,
  inPoints,
  outPoints,
  outMask,
  outInit,
  outInitPoints,
  table,
  scratchDir,
  executableDir)

if (returnCode != 0) {
  stop("Error in samplePoints, return code: ", returnCode)
}




Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)

pcum_in1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pcum_out1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pdif1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar_in1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar_out1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
density <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
dencum <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
rocplot <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
auc <- data.table(name=character(), val=numeric())
roc_dt <- data.table()
xrange <- data.table(start=numeric(),end=numeric())

for (i in 1:length(R4rasters)) {
  name <- R4rasters[[i]][[1]]
  
  d_in <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(table, "_", name, "_in.csv")))
  
  d_out <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(table, "_", name, "_out.csv")))
 
  sum_in <- d_in[, sum(Number)]
  sum_out <- d_out[, sum(Number)]
  
  pbar1[[i]] <- ggplot() +
    geom_bar(data=d_in, 
             aes(x = Mid, y = Number/sum_in, fill = "Inside"), 
             stat = "identity",
             alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_bar(data=d_out,
             aes(x = Mid, y = Number/sum_out, fill = "Outside"),
             stat = "identity",
             alpha = 0.6) +
    labs(x = name,
         y = "Proportion") +
    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "inside",
          legend.position.inside = c(0.9, 0.7),
          legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
          axis.title.x = element_blank(),
          axis.text.x = element_blank()) 
  
  pdif1[[i]] <- ggplot() +
    geom_line(data=d_in, 
              aes(x=Upper, y = CumulativeProp, color = "Inside"),
              linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_line(data=d_out,
              aes(x = Upper, y = CumulativeProp, color = "Outside"),
              linewidth = 1.2) +
    labs(x = name,
         y = "Cumulative Proportion") +
    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "inside",
          legend.position.inside = c(0.85, 0.4),
          legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
          axis.title.x = element_blank(),
          axis.text.x = element_blank())
  
# Calculate density as area of initiation zone per unit area of the study area (the masked area)
  den <- d_in[, .(Upper, Number)]
  den <- den[, Nout := d_out[, Number]]
  den <- den[Nout > 0, ]
  den <- den[, density := Number/Nout]

  xrange <- rbind(xrange, list(min(den$Upper),max(den$Upper)))
  
# Generate an ROC curve
  roc <- den[, .(density, Number, Nout)]
  roc <- roc[density > 0,]
  roc <- roc[order(-density)]
  roc <- roc[, tpr := rev(cumsum(Number))] # true positive rate
  tprmax <- max(roc$tpr)
  roc <- roc[, tpr := tpr/tprmax]
  roc <- roc[, fpr := rev(cumsum(Nout))] # false positive rate
  fprmax <- max(roc$fpr)
  roc <- roc[, fpr := fpr/fprmax]
  roc <- roc[, Index := as.factor(name)]
  roc_dt <- rbind(roc_dt,roc)

# Calculate area under the ROC curve  
  s = 0
  for (j in 1:(nrow(roc)-1)) {
    s_tpr <- (roc[j]$tpr + roc[j+1]$tpr) / 2
    s_fpr <- roc[j]$fpr - roc[j+1]$fpr
    s <- s + (s_tpr * s_fpr)
  }
  thisname <- R4rasters[[i]][1]
  auc <- rbind(auc, list(thisname, s))
  
  density[[i]] <- ggplot(data=den) + 
    geom_point(aes(x=Upper, y=density), shape = 1, size = 2.8) +
    geom_smooth(aes(x=Upper,y=density), method="loess",se=FALSE, span=0.2, color="black", linewidth = 1.) +
    labs(x = name, y = "Density") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim=c(xrange[i]$start,xrange[i]$end), ylim=c(0.,0.01))

  den <- den[order(density)]
  den <- den[, cumulative := cumsum(density)]  
  denmax <- max(den$cumulative)  
  den <- den[, cumulative := cumulative/denmax]
  
  dencum[[i]] <- ggplot(den, aes(x=Upper, y=cumulative)) +
    geom_point(shape = 20, size = 1.3) + 
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
    labs(title = name,
         x = "Density", 
         y = "Proportion of area")
  
  rocplot[[i]] <- ggplot(roc, aes(x=fpr, y=tpr)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_point(shape = 1, size = 4) +
    geom_segment(x=0,y=0,xend=1,yend=1, color="gray", linewidth=1.) +
    coord_cartesian(x=c(0,1)) +
    labs(title = name,
         subtitle = paste0("AUC = ", round(s, 3)),
         x = "False Positive Rate (Proportion of masked area)",
         y = "True Positive Rate (Proportion of initiation-zone area)")

}




Code
library(patchwork)

for (i in 1:length(R4rasters)) {
  text = paste0("Comparison of areas inside and outside of initiation zones for ", R4rasters[[i]][[1]])
  thisplot <- pbar1[[i]] / pdif1[[i]] / density[[i]] + plot_layout(nrow=3) +
    plot_annotation(title = paste0(text))
  plot(thisplot)
}








































































These plots show substantial differences in the degree to which these topographic indices can differentiate initiation zones. The index FoS_72 (Factor of safety calculated using saturation depth inferred from the contributing area for a 72-hour storm) exhibits the largest separation of the frequency distribution of values between the initiation zones and the masked portion of the study area, reflected in the largest range of density values.



Receiver operator curves (ROC) and area under the ROC curve (AUC) provide means to compare the ability of different predictors to differentiate areas with high landslide density from areas with lower landslide density.
For any of the indices, the integration of density over the study are gives the total area of initiation zones:
\[
A_{initiation} = \int_{A_T}\rho(s)ds \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n}a\rho_i
\tag{1}\]
where \(A_{initiation}\) is the cumulative area of all initiation zones, \(A_T\) is total study area, and \(\rho(s)\) is spatially variable landslide density. This integral is estimated by summing the value of \(\rho\) for each DEM cell (from the histograms above) times cell area \(a\). Performing the sum over ordered \(\rho\) values shows what proportion of the study area falls at or below (or above) a given \(\rho\) value. Limiting the sum only to mapped initiation zones shows what proportion of the total initiation-zone area falls at or below (or above) a given \(\rho\) value. Plotting the proportion of initiation-zone area as a function of proportion of total study area shows what proportion of study area contains a given proportion of the mapped initiation zones, ranked by landslide density.
These curves are referred to as Receiver-Operator-Characteristic (ROC) curves, or as Success-Rate Curves (Chung and Fabbri 2003). For an index where the frequency distribution of values within the initiation zones is distinctly different than the distribution over the entire study area (masked here to include only index values where landsliding is observed), the ROC curve will start out steeply, indicating that a large portion of the initiation-zone area is contained within a small proportion of the study area. If the two frequency distributions completely overlap, the ROC curve will follow the diagonal line from zero to one, shown by the dashed line in Figure 3.


Code
library(RColorBrewer)
library(forcats)

# Order indices by auc value
indexOrder <- c("FoS_72", "Gradient", "FoS_6", "PCA_6", "Aspect_30", "PCA_72", "Norm_30", "Log10Accum", "Mean_30", "Tan_30")
roc_dt <- roc_dt[, Index := factor(Index, levels=indexOrder)]
roc_curves <- ggplot(roc_dt, aes(x=fpr,y=tpr,color=Index)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1) +
  geom_segment(x=0,y=0,xend=1,yend=1, color="black", linewidth=1., linetype=3) +
  coord_cartesian(x=c(0,1), y=c(0,1)) +
  labs(title = "ROC curves",
       x = "False Positive Rate (Proportion of masked area)",
       y = "True Positive Rate (Proportion of initiation-zone area)") +
  theme(legend.position = "inside",
        legend.position.inside = c(.9,.4),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent")) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Spectral")

plot(roc_curves)








Figure 3: ROC curves for each topographic index





The area under an ROC curve (AUC) provides a measure of the degree to which an index can differentiate initiation zones from the general terrain. An AUC value of 0.5 indicates no discrimination ability, an AUC value of 1.0 indicates complete separation of the initiation-zone index values from the rest of the terrain.


Code
auc <- auc[, name := factor(unlist(name))]
aucplot <- ggplot(auc, aes(x=fct_reorder(name,-val),y=val)) + 
  geom_col(fill="lightgray", color="black") +
  coord_cartesian(y=c(0.5,0.85)) +
  labs(title="AUC for each of the topographic indices",
       x = "Topographic Index",
       y = "Area Under the Curve")

plot(aucplot)








Figure 4: AUC values for each topographic index





Gradient and FoS indices all provide the largest AUC values, followed by the contributing areas and hillslope aspect. The curvatures and total contributing area all have low AUC values, indicating little discriminating ability, as shown also by the high degree of overlap of the frequency distributions shown in the plots above.



This suggests that we could use gradient or one of the FoS models as relatively good predictors of future landslide location. However, it may be that density varies with certain combinations of indices: that there may be interactions among indices associated with variability in landslide density. For example, we may find that the density associated with FoS_72 varies with gradient, or with PCA_72, or with aspect, or with curvature, or with some combination of other indices. To explore potential interactions, we need to first ensure that the indices themselves are not correlated. For example, the calculation of FoS uses both gradient and contributing area. A change in FoS_72 value reflects changes in gradient and PCA_72 values, so including these other indices may provide little or no information that isn’t already represented by FoS_72. The next code chunk calculates correlations between each pair of index rasters, limited to the masked area.


Code
for (i in 1:nlyr(rstack)) {
  rstack[[i]] <- rstack[[i]] * mask }

corMatrix <- terra::layerCor(rstack, fun = "pearson", na.rm = TRUE)


I’ll use bar plots to examine the degree of correlation between indices. The largest AUC value is associated with FoS_72.

m <- corMatrix$correlation 
m_dt <- data.table(m, keep.rownames=TRUE) 
c_fos72 <- ggplot(m_dt, aes(x=fct_reorder(rn,-FoS_72),y=FoS_72)) + 
  geom_col(fill='lightgray',color='black',linewidth=0.5) +
  labs(title="Correlation with FoS_72",
       x = element_blank(),
       y = "Pierson's coefficient")

  geom_col(fill='lightgray',color='black',linewidth=0.5) +
  labs(title="Correlation with tangential curvature",
       x = element_blank(),

plot(c_plot)








Figure 5





FoS_72 is, of course, perfectly correlated with itself, but also highly correlated with FoS_6, Gradient, and the 6-hour partial contributing area indices. Correlation with curvature, total accumulation area, and aspect are all low, so interactions of these indices with FoS_72 may be worth exploring.
Directly binning covariate values works adequately for a single predictor. It may be, however, predictors act in combination to determine where landslides occur. Perhaps, for example, the density associated with a calculated FoS value varies with the upslope contributing area. To estimate the density of landslide initiation zones over multiple predictors requires binning in a multidimensional covariate data space. We may find we have insufficient data to adequately fill the bins. I’ll explore two alternatives for calculating landslide density as a function of one or more predictors: the use of spatial point process models, as used for presence-only data analysis, and subsampling of the predictors used to train a classification model.

title: Characterizing Initiation Locations
text:
The Tongass NF landslide inventory provides polygons indicating mapped landslide-scar boundaries. These include both the initiation, runout, and deposition zones.

library(data.table)

title: Characterizing Initiation Locations
text:
We can calculate a variety of topographic indices using the lidar DEM. As mentioned above, topographic attributes expected to be associated with landslide initiation zones include slope gradient, curvature, and flow accumulation. Gradient and curvature are scale-dependent, in that the value can vary with the length over which they are measured. These properties of the topography influence patterns and rates of movement for water and colluvium, thus influencing the depth of soil and the depth of soil saturation during rainstorm and snowmelt events. The pertinent length scales over which these patterns influence soil stability are reflected in the size distribution of the initiation zones. I calculated gradient and curvatures over a 30-meter length, equal to the mean of the initiation-zone widths (Figure 2).
The upslope flow-accumulation zone provides a relative measure of the amount of water flowing to the area represented by a DEM cell during rainstorms. Once subsurface flow through the soil reaches steady state, the discharge of water through a DEM cell is the upslope accumulation area times the rainfall intensity. This steady-state condition may never actually be achieved, so it may be informative to look at accumulation zones associated with storms of a fixed duration. These are calculated with program Partial, which I have prerun for a range of storm durations from 6 to 72 hours. This is an approximate solution using Darcy flow velocity for shallow subsurface flow, assuming that saturated water flow through the soil is parallel to the ground surface and ignoring the time for water to infiltrate to the saturated zone.
I also examine predictions of a simple physical model as a potential predictor, using the Factor of Safety FoS calculated with the infinite slope model assuming uniform soil properties. This model is an abstraction of actual physical conditions that ignores many factors pertinent to soil failure, so the modeled values may not provide a direct estimate of how landslide potential varies spatially. It does, however, provide a single-valued function that incorporates both slope gradient and flow accumulation. I’ll examine the FoS calculated using flow accumulation rasters generated for a 6-hour and 72-hour storm.
There are several options for exploring the degree to which different indices are associated with landslide initiation zones. A starting point is to look at the frequency distribution of index values both inside the initiation zones and over the entire study area. The degree to which these differ indicates the degree to which an index can differentiate initiation zones from the area in general. These distributions are plotted below using both bar plots and cumulative distributions.
To calculate these distributions, I masked the study area to include only those areas that fell within the range of gradient, FoS, and curvature values found within the initiation zones. Values outside of these zones have a landslide density of zero, based on this inventory. I want to focus on those areas of the landscape where landslide density is greater than zero.
For areas falling within any increment of an index value, the proportion of that area that is within the intiation zones provides a measure of landslide density for that increment. Program SamplePoints will extract these areas for each index. The following code chunks run SamplePoints and generate a series of plots showing histograms and cumulative frequency distributions of variable values both inside the initiation zones and over the entire study area. The landslide density associated with each binned inverval of variable values is also shown, with a smoothed line fit through the points. Density is calculated as the initiation-zone area divided by the total area within each interval range. The same vertical scale is used for all the density plots so that they can be compared directly.


Code
# Run sample points to build a raster masking zones outside the range of predictor values
# associated with landslide initiation zones. Edit input variables as needed
work <- "c:/work/data/wrangell/"
inRaster <- paste0(dataFolder,"init") # initiation zones, raster created by LS_poly above
areaPerSample <- 10000. # within initiation zones, one point every areaPerSample square meters
buffer_in <- 10. # buffer around sample points inside initiation zones, in meters
buffer_out <- 3. # buffer around sample points outside initiation zones
margin <- 1. # margin around initiation zones to preclude sample points close to the edge, in meters
ratio <- 3000. # ratio of sample points outside initiation zones to those within
nbins <- 100 # number of bins for building histograms

minPatch <- 100000.
inPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "inpoints1000")
outPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "outpoints") # used below for spatstat
outInit <- paste0(dataFolder, "initMod")
outInitPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "initPoints")
table <- paste0(dataFolder, "table")
scratchDir <- "c:/work/scratch"
executableDir <- "c:/work/sandbox/landslideutilities/projects/samplepoints/x64/release"

returnCode <- TerrainWorksUtils::samplePoints(
  inRaster,
  areaPerSample,
  buffer_in,
  buffer_out,
  margin,
  ratio,
  nbins,
  R4rasters,
  I4rasters,
  minPatch,
  inPoints,
  outPoints,
  outMask,
  outInit,
  outInitPoints,
  table,
  scratchDir,
  executableDir)

if (returnCode != 0) {
  stop("Error in samplePoints, return code: ", returnCode)
}




Code
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)

pcum_in1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pcum_out1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pdif1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar_in1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar_out1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
density <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
dencum <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
rocplot <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
auc <- data.table(name=character(), val=numeric())
roc_dt <- data.table()
xrange <- data.table(start=numeric(),end=numeric())

for (i in 1:length(R4rasters)) {
  name <- R4rasters[[i]][[1]]
  
  d_in <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(table, "_", name, "_in.csv")))
  
  d_out <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(table, "_", name, "_out.csv")))
 
  sum_in <- d_in[, sum(Number)]
  sum_out <- d_out[, sum(Number)]
  
  pbar1[[i]] <- ggplot() +
    geom_bar(data=d_in, 
             aes(x = Mid, y = Number/sum_in, fill = "Inside"), 
             stat = "identity",
             alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_bar(data=d_out,
             aes(x = Mid, y = Number/sum_out, fill = "Outside"),
             stat = "identity",
             alpha = 0.6) +
    labs(x = name,
         y = "Proportion") +
    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "inside",
          legend.position.inside = c(0.9, 0.7),
          legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
          axis.title.x = element_blank(),
          axis.text.x = element_blank()) 
  
  pdif1[[i]] <- ggplot() +
    geom_line(data=d_in, 
              aes(x=Upper, y = CumulativeProp, color = "Inside"),
              linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_line(data=d_out,
              aes(x = Upper, y = CumulativeProp, color = "Outside"),
              linewidth = 1.2) +
    labs(x = name,
         y = "Cumulative Proportion") +
    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "inside",
          legend.position.inside = c(0.85, 0.4),
          legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
          axis.title.x = element_blank(),
          axis.text.x = element_blank())
  
# Calculate density as area of initiation zone per unit area of the study area (the masked area)
  den <- d_in[, .(Upper, Number)]
  den <- den[, Nout := d_out[, Number]]
  den <- den[Nout > 0, ]
  den <- den[, density := Number/Nout]

  xrange <- rbind(xrange, list(min(den$Upper),max(den$Upper)))
  
# Generate an ROC curve
  roc <- den[, .(density, Number, Nout)]
  roc <- roc[density > 0,]
  roc <- roc[order(-density)]
  roc <- roc[, tpr := rev(cumsum(Number))] # true positive rate
  tprmax <- max(roc$tpr)
  roc <- roc[, tpr := tpr/tprmax]
  roc <- roc[, fpr := rev(cumsum(Nout))] # false positive rate
  fprmax <- max(roc$fpr)
  roc <- roc[, fpr := fpr/fprmax]
  roc <- roc[, Index := as.factor(name)]
  roc_dt <- rbind(roc_dt,roc)

# Calculate area under the ROC curve  
  s = 0
  for (j in 1:(nrow(roc)-1)) {
    s_tpr <- (roc[j]$tpr + roc[j+1]$tpr) / 2
    s_fpr <- roc[j]$fpr - roc[j+1]$fpr
    s <- s + (s_tpr * s_fpr)
  }
  thisname <- R4rasters[[i]][1]
  auc <- rbind(auc, list(thisname, s))
  
  density[[i]] <- ggplot(data=den) + 
    geom_point(aes(x=Upper, y=density), shape = 1, size = 2.8) +
    geom_smooth(aes(x=Upper,y=density), method="loess",se=FALSE, span=0.2, color="black", linewidth = 1.) +
    labs(x = name, y = "Density") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim=c(xrange[i]$start,xrange[i]$end), ylim=c(0.,0.01))

  den <- den[order(density)]
  den <- den[, cumulative := cumsum(density)]  
  denmax <- max(den$cumulative)  
  den <- den[, cumulative := cumulative/denmax]
  
  dencum[[i]] <- ggplot(den, aes(x=Upper, y=cumulative)) +
    geom_point(shape = 20, size = 1.3) + 
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
    labs(title = name,
         x = "Density", 
         y = "Proportion of area")
  
  rocplot[[i]] <- ggplot(roc, aes(x=fpr, y=tpr)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_point(shape = 1, size = 4) +
    geom_segment(x=0,y=0,xend=1,yend=1, color="gray", linewidth=1.) +
    coord_cartesian(x=c(0,1)) +
    labs(title = name,
         subtitle = paste0("AUC = ", round(s, 3)),
         x = "False Positive Rate (Proportion of masked area)",
         y = "True Positive Rate (Proportion of initiation-zone area)")

}




Code
library(patchwork)

for (i in 1:length(R4rasters)) {
  text = paste0("Comparison of areas inside and outside of initiation zones for ", R4rasters[[i]][[1]])
  thisplot <- pbar1[[i]] / pdif1[[i]] / density[[i]] + plot_layout(nrow=3) +
    plot_annotation(title = paste0(text))
  plot(thisplot)
}








































































These plots show substantial differences in the degree to which these topographic indices can differentiate initiation zones. The index FoS_72 (Factor of safety calculated using saturation depth inferred from the contributing area for a 72-hour storm) exhibits the largest separation of the frequency distribution of values between the initiation zones and the masked portion of the study area, reflected in the largest range of density values.

title: Characterizing Initiation Locations
text:
Receiver operator curves (ROC) and area under the ROC curve (AUC) provide means to compare the ability of different predictors to differentiate areas with high landslide density from areas with lower landslide density.
For any of the indices, the integration of density over the study are gives the total area of initiation zones:
\[
A_{initiation} = \int_{A_T}\rho(s)ds \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n}a\rho_i
\tag{1}\]
where \(A_{initiation}\) is the cumulative area of all initiation zones, \(A_T\) is total study area, and \(\rho(s)\) is spatially variable landslide density. This integral is estimated by summing the value of \(\rho\) for each DEM cell (from the histograms above) times cell area \(a\). Performing the sum over ordered \(\rho\) values shows what proportion of the study area falls at or below (or above) a given \(\rho\) value. Limiting the sum only to mapped initiation zones shows what proportion of the total initiation-zone area falls at or below (or above) a given \(\rho\) value. Plotting the proportion of initiation-zone area as a function of proportion of total study area shows what proportion of study area contains a given proportion of the mapped initiation zones, ranked by landslide density.
These curves are referred to as Receiver-Operator-Characteristic (ROC) curves, or as Success-Rate Curves (Chung and Fabbri 2003). For an index where the frequency distribution of values within the initiation zones is distinctly different than the distribution over the entire study area (masked here to include only index values where landsliding is observed), the ROC curve will start out steeply, indicating that a large portion of the initiation-zone area is contained within a small proportion of the study area. If the two frequency distributions completely overlap, the ROC curve will follow the diagonal line from zero to one, shown by the dashed line in Figure 3.


Code
library(RColorBrewer)
library(forcats)

# Order indices by auc value
indexOrder <- c("FoS_72", "Gradient", "FoS_6", "PCA_6", "Aspect_30", "PCA_72", "Norm_30", "Log10Accum", "Mean_30", "Tan_30")
roc_dt <- roc_dt[, Index := factor(Index, levels=indexOrder)]
roc_curves <- ggplot(roc_dt, aes(x=fpr,y=tpr,color=Index)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1) +
  geom_segment(x=0,y=0,xend=1,yend=1, color="black", linewidth=1., linetype=3) +
  coord_cartesian(x=c(0,1), y=c(0,1)) +
  labs(title = "ROC curves",
       x = "False Positive Rate (Proportion of masked area)",
       y = "True Positive Rate (Proportion of initiation-zone area)") +
  theme(legend.position = "inside",
        legend.position.inside = c(.9,.4),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent")) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Spectral")

plot(roc_curves)








Figure 3: ROC curves for each topographic index





The area under an ROC curve (AUC) provides a measure of the degree to which an index can differentiate initiation zones from the general terrain. An AUC value of 0.5 indicates no discrimination ability, an AUC value of 1.0 indicates complete separation of the initiation-zone index values from the rest of the terrain.


Code
auc <- auc[, name := factor(unlist(name))]
aucplot <- ggplot(auc, aes(x=fct_reorder(name,-val),y=val)) + 
  geom_col(fill="lightgray", color="black") +
  coord_cartesian(y=c(0.5,0.85)) +
  labs(title="AUC for each of the topographic indices",
       x = "Topographic Index",
       y = "Area Under the Curve")

plot(aucplot)








Figure 4: AUC values for each topographic index





Gradient and FoS indices all provide the largest AUC values, followed by the contributing areas and hillslope aspect. The curvatures and total contributing area all have low AUC values, indicating little discriminating ability, as shown also by the high degree of overlap of the frequency distributions shown in the plots above.

title: Characterizing Initiation Locations
text:
This suggests that we could use gradient or one of the FoS models as relatively good predictors of future landslide location. However, it may be that density varies with certain combinations of indices: that there may be interactions among indices associated with variability in landslide density. For example, we may find that the density associated with FoS_72 varies with gradient, or with PCA_72, or with aspect, or with curvature, or with some combination of other indices. To explore potential interactions, we need to first ensure that the indices themselves are not correlated. For example, the calculation of FoS uses both gradient and contributing area. A change in FoS_72 value reflects changes in gradient and PCA_72 values, so including these other indices may provide little or no information that isn’t already represented by FoS_72. The next code chunk calculates correlations between each pair of index rasters, limited to the masked area.


Code
for (i in 1:nlyr(rstack)) {
  rstack[[i]] <- rstack[[i]] * mask }

corMatrix <- terra::layerCor(rstack, fun = "pearson", na.rm = TRUE)

Perhaps, for example, the density associated with a calculated FoS value varies with the upslope contributing area. To estimate the density of landslide initiation zones over multiple predictors requires binning in a multidimensional covariate data space. We may find we have insufficient data to adequately fill the bins. I’ll explore two alternatives for calculating landslide density as a function of one or more predictors: the use of spatial point process models, as used for presence-only data analysis, and subsampling of the predictors used to train a classification model.

title: Initiation
text:
Introduction
How can we effectively characterize the potential for landslide initiation? A landslide inventory shows where landslides have occurred over some period of time. We use the inventory to identify conditions spatially and temporally associated with past landslide occurrences. We assume that the landslides in the inventory represent some proportion of the population of all possible landslides and expect that landslides in the future will be associated with similar conditions. We can characterize these associations in terms of density: the number (or area or volume) of observed landslides per unit basin area. We seek to determine how landslide density varies spatially as a function of measurable attributes of the terrain and weather associated with the landslides observed. In most cases, we focus on the terrain because we do not have reliable methods for measuring attributes of the weather associated with landslide occurrence.
Once we have functions describing landslide density in terms of terrain attributes, we can create maps of landslide density based on those attributes. If done well, we should find that most of the observed landslide initiation zones fall within areas of high modeled landslide density, but not all the initiation zones, because some proportion of the landslides will have occurred in unusual locations. A low modeled density indicates conditions associated with a smaller proportion of the observed landslides. Importantly, we will also have zones of high modeled landslide density where there are no observed landslides in our inventory. We expect that the inventory represents only a portion of the entire population of all possible landslides. Areas of high modeled density indicate those zones with conditions similar to the conditions where landslides were observed. We expect that these will be sites of landslide occurrence in the future.
Within the boundaries of the basin or study-area where the landslide inventory was collected, if we integrate the modeled density over that area, the integral will give the number (or area or volume, depending on how the landslide initiation zones were characterized) of landslides within that boundary. The number (area, volume) of inventoried landslides, however, depends on the time period over which the inventory was collected and the landslide-triggering storms that occurred during that period. Density can, therefore, only provide a relative measure of how landslide potential varies over the landscape, showing where conditions associated with a greater or lesser proportion of the landslides in the inventory exist.
To provide an absolute measure of landslide potential, we can translate the density of landslides to the proportion of landslides. Then our maps go from indicating the number (or area or volume) of landslides in the inventory associated with particular terrain attributes to the proportion of landslides in the inventory associated with those attributes. This provides a testable prediction. For any future landslide inventory, we can predict what proportion of observed landslides will occur within any particular portion of a basin or study area. We can then, for example, delineate those locations from which we expect a certain proportion (e.g., 20%) of all landslides will occur, ranked from high to low landslide density.
The goal here is to find functions relating landslide density to terrain attributes. For a single attribute, we can measure density directly (Chung and Fabbri 1999). Over any increment of an attribute value (e.g., 70% ≤ Gradient < 80%), the density over that increment is the number (area, volume) of landslides within that increment divided by the total area within the study boundary within that increment. This measure of density provides a direct method for relating terrain attributes to known landslide occurrences and forms the basis for characterizing the spatial distribution of points (Adrian Baddeley, Ege Rubak, and Rolf Turner 2016), as we will explore below.
There are also a variety of classification modeling strategies available: logistic regression, random forest, etc., that might provide an alternative perspective of how landslide locations are related to terrain attributes. These models require that we extract a sample of points from both the initiation zones and the rest of the study area. The attributes associated with those points are then used to characterize conditions associated with the initiation zones. This characterization is provided as a probability that any location is within an initiation zone.





References

Adrian Baddeley, Ege Rubak, and Rolf Turner. 2016. Spatial Point Patterns, Methodology and Applications with r. CRC Press, Taylor & Francis Group.


Chung, Chang-Jo F., and G. Fabbri. 1999. “Probabilistic Prediction Models for Landslide Hazard Mapping.” Photogrammetric Engineering & Remote Sensing 65 (12): 1389–99.

title: FoS
text:
This section describes the infinite slope approximation, following Skempton and DeLory (1957). See this article in the National High School Journal of Science for a nice description of the infinite slope model.
We define the Factor of Safety (FoS) as the ratio of forces acting to hold soil in place to those acting to move soil downslope. FoS values greater than one indicate stability, less than one indicate failure. For a cohesionless, constant-thickness, isotropic, uniform soil layer on an infinite slope with steady-state surface-parallel saturated subsurface flow, forces acting to hold soil in place are given by the effective normal stress \(\sigma_n'\) at the base of the soil column (i.e., the soil-bedrock interface) multiplied by \(\tan\phi\), where \(\phi\) is the friction angle of the soil. The effective stress is given by the proportion of the weight of the soil column directed normal to the base of the soil layer minus the pore pressure at the base. For a soil layer of depth \(z,\) the weight of a unit length and width soil column is \(\gamma_s(z\cos\theta),\) where \(\gamma_s\) is the unit weight of soil (assumed spatially uniform) and \(\theta\) is the slope of the base of the soil layer (measured from horizontal), which is assumed parallel to the ground surface (the soil layer has uniform thickness). The portion of that weight directed normal to the base of the soil layer is \[\sigma_n = \gamma_s(z\cos\theta)\cos\theta.\]
For a saturated zone of vertical depth \(h\) within the soil layer, with seepage directed parallel to the surface of the ground surface, the pore pressure \(u\) at the base of the soil layer is \[u = \gamma_w(h\cos\theta)\cos\theta.\]
The effective stress at the base of the soil layer is \(\sigma_n' = \sigma_n - u,\) so the force acting to hold soil in place is: \[Force\;holding\;soil\;in\;place = (\sigma_n - u)\tan\phi = \gamma_s z \cos\theta^2 - \gamma_w h \cos\theta^2.\]
We can determine the depth of saturation \(h\) based on the discharge through the soil column. That discharge is equal to the surface area of the flow tube exiting the soil column, measured normal to the direction of water flow, times the Darcy velocity of flow through that surface. The flow tube defined by flow exiting the column has width \(b\) as it flows out of the column, where \(b\) is the length of the contour line (intersection of the equipotential head surface with the ground surface) crossed by flow exiting the cell, and height \(h\cos\theta\) measured normal to the direction of water flow. The Darcy velocity of flow through the flow tube is given by \(K\sin\theta,\) where \(K\) is saturated hydraulic conductivity of the soil, assumed spatially uniform. So discharge \(q\) out of the soil column is \(q = b(h\cos\theta)K\sin\theta\). For steady-state rainfall of intensity \(I,\) in depth per unit time, discharge out of the soil column is \(IA,\) where \(A\) is the contributing area (measured horizontally) for subsurface flow from infiltrating rainfall to the soil column. From this, we can define the vertical depth of saturation as
\[h = \frac{IA}{bK\sin\theta\cos\theta}.
\]
The force tending to move soil downslope is given by the portion of the soil-column weight directed parallel to the base of the soil layer \(\gamma_s (z \cos\theta) \sin\theta\). So the factor of safety (FoS) for the soil layer is defined as
\[
FoS = \frac{(\gamma_sz\cos^2\theta - \frac{IA\gamma_w\cos^2\theta)}{Kb\sin\theta\cos\theta})\tan\phi}{\gamma_sz\sin \theta \cos\theta} = \frac{(\gamma_sz - \frac{IA\gamma_w)}{Kb\sin\theta\cos\theta})\tan\phi}{\gamma_sz\tan\theta}
\tag{1}\]
If we hold soil properties \(\gamma_s, \gamma_w, z, \phi\), and \(K\) constant, and assume steady state rainfall intensity \(I\), then FoS is a function solely of topographic attributes \(b\) and \(\theta\) and of the contributing area \(A\). We measure \(b\) and \(\theta\) from the DEM. Contributing area is estimated as a function of storm duration, assuming a spatially constant saturated hydraulic conductivity.
The use of a steady-state condition and infinite slope to derive these equations is not realistic. Clearly, slopes are not planar and infinite, soil is not uniform, and rainfall is not steady state. More realistic models can be derived (Iverson 2000), though with more parameters the values of which must be inferred. However, the infinite slope model has been employed successfully as an indicator of potential instability (e.g., Montgomery and Dietrich 1994 and all subsequent SHALSTAB studies; e.g., Dietrich, Bellugi, and Asua 2001) and, as used here, it provides a single-valued measure of potential topographic influences on slope stability via values of \(b, \theta\,\) and \(A\). This is confounded by correlations of soil properties with topography. We may be able to counter this to some extent by including mapped soil types in the model. We may also experiment with topographically based models of soil depth.
Figure 1 below shows the behavior of Equation 1 over a range of slope gradients for two contributing-area values, with all soil properties and rainfall intensity held constant. Figure 2 shows the behavior when soil depth changes. We use the DEM to estimate effects of changing gradient and contributing area on slope stability, but for now we are holding soil depth constant.


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(data.table)

gamma_s <- 1.8 # unit weight of soil normalized by unit weight of water
z <- 1.0 # soil layer depth measured vertically
tanPhi <- 0.7
K <- 1.0
b <- 1.0
tantheta <- seq(from=0.4,to=1.5,length.out=100)
theta <- atan(tantheta)
costheta <- cos(theta)
sintheta <- sin(theta)

I <- 0.01
A <- 5.
u <- I*A/(K*b*sintheta*costheta)
FoS1 <- (gamma_s*z - u)*tanPhi/(gamma_s*z*tantheta)

I <- 0.01
A <- 15.
u <- I*A/(K*b*sintheta*costheta)
FoS2 <- (gamma_s*z - u)*tanPhi/(gamma_s*z*tantheta)

FoSdata <- data.table(tantheta,FoS1,FoS2)
long <- melt(FoSdata, id='tantheta')
thisplot <- ggplot(long, aes(x=tantheta, y=value)) + 
  geom_line(aes(colour=variable)) +
  labs(
    x = "Gradient = Tan(theta)",
    y = "FoS",
    colour = "Contributing Area (sq m)",
    title = "Change of FoS with surface gradient",
    subtitle = "z=1.0, tan(phi)=0.7, K=1.0, b=1.0, I=0.01",
    caption = "Increasing slope or contributing area reduces FoS"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "inside", legend.position.inside = c(0.6, 0.8)) +
  scale_color_discrete(labels = c("A=5.", "A=15."))
plot(thisplot)








Figure 1







Code
gamma_s <- 1.8 # unit weight of soil normalized by unit weight of water
z <- seq(from=0.1, to=1.5, length.out=100) # soil layer depth measured vertically
tanPhi <- 0.7
K <- 1.0
b <- 1.0

tantheta <- 0.7
theta <- atan(tantheta)
costheta <- cos(theta)
sintheta <- sin(theta)
I <- 0.01
A <- 5.
u <- I*A/(K*b*sintheta*costheta)
FoS1 <- (gamma_s*z - u)*tanPhi/(gamma_s*z*tantheta)

tantheta <- 0.8
theta <- atan(tantheta)
costheta <- cos(theta)
sintheta <- sin(theta)
I <- 0.01
A <- 5.
u <- I*A/(K*b*sintheta*costheta)
FoS2 <- (gamma_s*z - u)*tanPhi/(gamma_s*z*tantheta)

FoSdata <- data.table(z,FoS1,FoS2)
long <- melt(FoSdata, id='z')
thisplot <- ggplot(long, aes(x=z, y=value)) + 
  geom_line(aes(colour=variable)) +
  labs(
    x = "Soil Depth (m)",
    y = "FoS",
    colour = "Surface Gradient",
    title = "Change of FoS with soil depth",
    subtitle = "K=1.0, b=1.0, I=0.01",
    caption = "Thinner soils and steeper slopes reduces FoS"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "inside", legend.position.inside = c(0.8, 0.2)) +
  scale_color_discrete(labels = c("70%", "80%"))
plot(thisplot)








Figure 2





Figure 3 below shows the distribution of FoS values both within mapped landslide initiation zones and over all of Wrangell Island. This example used the contributing area calculated for a 6-hour-duraction storm. The initiation zones are distinguished by lower FoS values, indicating that this estimate of a factor of safety, based solely on topographic attributes, provides a useful means of identifying areas prone to landslide initiation.


Code
library(terra)

# Get the FoS raster created with program PartialAccum for a 5-hour-duration storm.
FoS_pca6 <- terra::rast("c:/work/data/wrangell/fos_pca6.flt")
f <- as.data.table(FoS_pca6) 
# Get the FoS values at the inventoried initiation points
pnts <- terra::vect("c:/work/data/wrangell/LS_pnts.shp")
ls <- terra::extract(FoS_pca6,pnts)
# Filter the raster FoS values to include only those less than the maximum 
# point value.
#maxls <- max(ls$fos_pca6)
fsub <- subset(f, fos_pca6 <= 1.25)
f1 <- as.data.table(fsub)

p1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_density(data=f1, aes(x=fos_pca6, fill = 'DEM'), alpha=0.5) +
  geom_density(data=ls, aes(x=fos_pca6, fill = 'Landslides'), alpha=0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(name = "", values = c(DEM = 'blue', Landslides = 'red')) +
  labs(
    x = "Factor of Safety",
    y = "Density",
    title = "Wrangell",
    subtitle = "Storm duration = 6hr") +
  theme(legend.position = "inside", legend.position.inside = c(0.6, 0.8)) +
  coord_cartesian(x=c(0,1.2))
  
p1








Figure 3









References

Dietrich, W. E., Dino Bellugi, and R. R. de Asua. 2001. “Validation of the Shallow Landslide Model, SHALSTAB, for Forest Management.” In, edited by M. S. Wigmosta and S. J. Burges, 195–227. Washington, D.C.: American Geophysical Union.


Iverson, Richard M. 2000. “Landslide Triggering by Rain Infiltration.” Water Resources Research 36 (7): 1897–1910. https://doi.org/10.1029/2000wr900090.


Montgomery, David R., and William E. Dietrich. 1994. “A Physically Based Model for the Topographic Control on Shallow Landsliding.” Water Resources Research 30 (4): 1153–71. https://doi.org/10.1029/93wr02979.


Skempton, A. W., and F. A. deLory. 1957. “Stability of Natural Slopes in London Clay.”

title: About
text:
This website documents my ongoing efforts to develop a workflow for empirical evaluation of susceptibility to landslide initiation and runout in Southeast Alaska. This work is funded by the Tongass National Forest and relies on the landslide inventory collected by the Tongass National Forest. My current efforts are focused on Wrangell Island, illustrated with the examples and discussion in the pages included on this website.
This is a work in progress, so what you see posted now will evolve as we work through this analysis and receive feedback from reviewers like you.

title: Landslide Susceptibility in Southeast Alaska
text:
With support and collaboration from the Tongass National Forest, TerrainWorks has developed and implemented empirical models using remotely sensed data to provide estimates for susceptibility to landslide initiation and runout for sites in Southeast Alaska. Reports are available that describe these efforts for Sitka and Prince of Wales Island. We continue those efforts here with Wrangell and Mitkof Islands.
With each study, we seek to expand our use of available information and statistical modeling tools to improve on our ability to identify and rank areas susceptible to landslide initiation and runout. This is the first study where we share our ongoing efforts using a website. The intent of this website is both to document our efforts and to provide opportunities for feedback over the course of the study.
This is a work in progress, so what you see posted now will evolve as we work through this analysis and receive feedback from reviewers like you.

title: Characterizing Initiation Locations
text:
The issue now is, how to select the sample of points? How many, and how should they be divided between the initiation zones and everywhere else? Given that the initiation zones may cover a fraction of a percent of the study area, it is not immediately obvious how to divvy up the points. An assumption inherent in these classification methods is that the value at any sample point is independent of the value at any other point. Because these methods are ultimately determining proportions (e.g., what proportion of sampled points over a gradient interval of 0.7 to 0.8 fell within initiation zones), if some points are essentially sampling the same location (e.g., the same landslide scar), the calculated proportions will be biased towards values at those locations. How do we ensure that the sampled points provide independent values? Finally, how do our choices about sample selection affect the modeled probabilities?


How far apart do the sample points need to be to ensure that each provides a measure of terrain attributes independent of the values at any other point? We can look at the degree of autocorrelation for topographic attributes using, e.g., a semivariogram, as we do below, but what does that actually tell us?
Consider a long, planar slope. The elevation changes in a regular fashion along the fall line, but not at all along a contour. The gradient (measured along the fall line) is about the same everywhere on the slope, so the degree of correlation is high. The gradient will decrease at the ridge crest and the base of the slope, so the range on the semivariogram should be about the length of the hillslope. Yet, we may find multiple, separate landslides initiating within lesser distances of each other on the slope. What terrain attributes might be affecting landslide initiation? If we exclude the ridge and base of the slope from calculation of the semivariance, we may find a smaller range (and sill) indicative of minor variations in gradient along the slope. Is this the appropriate separation distance to use?
Given our reliance on remotely sensed data, we cannot fully assess the factors that influence landslide initiation and it is probable that some of these factors are not visible with the data we have. These invisible-to-use factors may vary over length scales less than the variations visible in our remotely sensed data, even with high-resolution lidar-derived topography. The size distribution of the landslide scars themselves may offer some insight. Here are boxplots and cumulative frequency distributions of the estimated width of the initiation zones of the 244 landslide polygons used from the Tongass National Forest landslide inventory on Wrangell Island. Width is estimated both as the square root of the initiation-zone surface area and from the average width of the entire landslide polygon.
Both measures suggest that about 90% of all initiation zones are 50 m or less in width. Let’s look at some potential predictors. Program samplePoints will read the specified rasters of predictors, find the range of predictor values that fall within the initiation-zone polygons, and create a mask file to exclude areas outside those ranges. This is similar to excluding the ridge top and valley floor with looking at gradient values in the planar-slope example described above. We are examining only those areas with the range of predictor values where landslide initiations were observed. The code chunks below will run samplePoints to create a mask raster and then use the cv_spatial_autocor function from the blockCV package to calculate semivariograms for each predictor raster. The semivariogram will indicate the range of spatial autocorrelation for each predictor.


Code
library(blockCV)
library(automap)
library(sf)
library(terra)
library(stringr)
library(TerrainWorksUtils)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(data.table)

dataFolder <- "c:/work/data/wrangell/"

rasterList <- read.csv(paste0(dataFolder, "R4rasterList.csv"), )
R4rasters <- vector("list",nrow(rasterList)-1)

for (i in 1:nrow(rasterList)) {

  if (str_detect(rasterList[i,1], "MASK")) {
    outMask <- rasterList[i,2]
  } else {
    r <- vector("list", 6)
    for (j in 1:6) {
      r[[j]] <- trimws(rasterList[i,j])
    }
    R4rasters[[i]] <- r
  }
}

for (i in 1:length(R4rasters)) {
  r <- terra::rast(paste0(R4rasters[[i]][2],".flt"))
  names(r) <- R4rasters[[i]][1]
  if (i == 1) {
    rstack <- r
  } else {
    rstack <- c(rstack, r)
  }
}

rasterList <- read.csv(paste0(dataFolder, "I4rasterList.csv"))
I4rasters <- vector("list", nrow(rasterList))

for (i in 1:nrow(rasterList)) {
  r <- vector("list",3)
  r[[1]] <- trimws(rasterList[i,1])
  r[[2]] <- trimws(rasterList[i,2])
  r[[3]] <- trimws(rasterList[i,3])
  I4rasters[[i]] <- r
}

# Run sample points to build a raster masking zones outside the range of predictor values
# associated with landslide initiation zones. Edit input variables as needed
work <- "c:/work/data/wrangell/"
inRaster <- paste0(dataFolder,"init") # initiation zones, raster created by LS_poly above
areaPerSample <- 10000. # within initiation zones, one point every areaPerSample square meters
buffer_in <- 10. # buffer around sample points inside initiation zones, in meters
buffer_out <- 3. # buffer around sample points outside initiation zones
margin <- 1. # margin around initiation zones to preclude sample points close to the edge, in meters
ratio <- 3000. # ratio of sample points outside initiation zones to those within
nbins <- 100 # number of bins for building histograms

minPatch <- 100000.
inPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "inpoints1000")
outPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "outpoints") # used below for spatstat
outInit <- paste0(dataFolder, "initMod")
outInitPoints <- paste0(dataFolder, "initPoints")
table <- paste0(dataFolder, "table")
scratchDir <- "c:/work/scratch"
executableDir <- "c:/work/sandbox/landslideutilities/projects/samplepoints/x64/release"

returnCode <- TerrainWorksUtils::samplePoints(
  inRaster,
  areaPerSample,
  buffer_in,
  buffer_out,
  margin,
  ratio,
  nbins,
  R4rasters,
  I4rasters,
  minPatch,
  inPoints,
  outPoints,
  outMask,
  outInit,
  outInitPoints,
  table,
  scratchDir,
  executableDir)

if (returnCode != 0) {
  stop("Error in samplePoints, return code: ", returnCode)
}

pcum_in1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pcum_out1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pdif1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar_in1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
pbar_out1 <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
density <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
dencum <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
rocplot <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(R4rasters))
auc <- data.table(name=character(), val=numeric())
roc_dt <- data.table()
xrange <- data.table(start=numeric(),end=numeric())

for (i in 1:length(R4rasters)) {
  name <- R4rasters[[i]][[1]]
  
  d_in <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(table, "_", name, "_in.csv")))
  
  d_out <- as.data.table(read.csv(paste0(table, "_", name, "_out.csv")))
 
  sum_in <- d_in[, sum(Number)]
  sum_out <- d_out[, sum(Number)]
  
  pbar1[[i]] <- ggplot() +
    geom_bar(data=d_in, 
             aes(x = Mid, y = Number/sum_in, fill = "Inside"), 
             stat = "identity",
             alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_bar(data=d_out,
             aes(x = Mid, y = Number/sum_out, fill = "Outside"),
             stat = "identity",
             alpha = 0.6) +
    labs(x = name,
         y = "Proportion") +
    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "inside",
          legend.position.inside = c(0.9, 0.8)) 
  
  pdif1[[i]] <- ggplot() +
    geom_line(data=d_in, 
              aes(x=Upper, y = CumulativeProp, color = "Inside"),
              linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_line(data=d_out,
              aes(x = Upper, y = CumulativeProp, color = "Outside"),
              linewidth = 1.2) +
    labs(x = name,
         y = "Cumulative Proportion") +
    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "inside",
          legend.position.inside = c(0.85, 0.2))
  
# Calculate density as area of initiation zone per unit area of the study area (the masked area)
  den <- d_in[, .(Upper, Number)]
  den <- den[, Nout := d_out[, Number]]
  den <- den[Nout > 0, ]
  den <- den[, density := Number/Nout]

  xrange <- rbind(xrange, list(min(den$Upper),max(den$Upper)))
  
# Generate an ROC curve
  roc <- den[, .(density, Number, Nout)]
  roc <- roc[density > 0,]
  roc <- roc[order(-density)]
  roc <- roc[, tpr := rev(cumsum(Number))] # true positive rate
  tprmax <- max(roc$tpr)
  roc <- roc[, tpr := tpr/tprmax]
  roc <- roc[, fpr := rev(cumsum(Nout))] # false positive rate
  fprmax <- max(roc$fpr)
  roc <- roc[, fpr := fpr/fprmax]
  roc <- roc[, Index := as.factor(name)]
  roc_dt <- rbind(roc_dt,roc)

# Calculate area under the ROC curve  
  s = 0
  for (j in 1:(nrow(roc)-1)) {
    s_tpr <- (roc[j]$tpr + roc[j+1]$tpr) / 2
    s_fpr <- roc[j]$fpr - roc[j+1]$fpr
    s <- s + (s_tpr * s_fpr)
  }
  thisname <- R4rasters[[i]][1]
  auc <- rbind(auc, list(thisname, s))
  
  density[[i]] <- ggplot(data=den) + 
    geom_point(aes(x=Upper, y=density), shape = 1, size = 2.8) +
    geom_smooth(aes(x=Upper,y=density), method="loess",se=FALSE, span=0.2, color="black", linewidth = 1.) +
    labs(x = name, y = "Density") +
    coord_cartesian(xlim=c(xrange[i]$start,xrange[i]$end))

  den <- den[order(density)]
  den <- den[, cumulative := cumsum(density)]  
  denmax <- max(den$cumulative)  
  den <- den[, cumulative := cumulative/denmax]
  
  dencum[[i]] <- ggplot(den, aes(x=Upper, y=cumulative)) +
    geom_point(shape = 20, size = 1.3) + 
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
    labs(title = name,
         x = "Density", 
         y = "Proportion of area")
  
  rocplot[[i]] <- ggplot(roc, aes(x=fpr, y=tpr)) +
    geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_point(shape = 1, size = 4) +
    geom_segment(x=0,y=0,xend=1,yend=1, color="gray", linewidth=1.) +
    coord_cartesian(x=c(0,1)) +
    labs(title = name,
         subtitle = paste0("AUC = ", round(s, 3)),
         x = "False Positive Rate (Proportion of masked area)",
         y = "True Positive Rate (Proportion of initiation-zone area)")

}




Code
# Build a semivariogram for each predictor.
var <- cv_spatial_autocor(r=rstack, num_sample=100000)

  |                                                                      |   0%
  |                                                                            
  |=======                                                               |  10%

Code
var$range_table


       layers        range         sill
6      Tan_30     311.9605 1.318585e-06
10 Log10Accum     414.6621 2.227805e-01
7     Norm_30     562.5731 2.804992e-06
8     Mean_30     599.7623 2.357885e-06
9   Aspect_30     835.7722 2.313318e+00
1    Gradient     932.1226 5.544100e-02
5      PCA_72     971.7154 1.625592e+01
4       PCA_6    1222.0964 1.141343e-01
2       FoS_6  565210.6766 2.169860e+04
3      FoS_72 1364964.5638 2.947310e+04


Gradient was calculated over a diameter of 30 meters, thus smoothing variations over length scales less than that. The range of the semivariogram for this measure of gradient is about 300 meters, which is around the midpoint of hillslope lengths included within the mask raster. The two flow accumulation rasters, PCA_5 and PCA_72, indicate the approximate contributing area for each DEM cell for a 5 and 72 hour storm, assuming a saturated hydraulic conductivity of 1 m/sec and ignoring infiltration time. PCA_5 is highly correlated with gradient and has a similar semivariogram range. PCA_72 has flow concentrations within channels and exhibits much larger variability than PCA_5, indicated by its larger range and sill. The factor of safety values (FoS) are derived from the gradient and flow accumulation values. Despite the large differences between PCA_5 and PCA_72, the FoS rasters based on these exhibit similar patterns of variability with nearly equal ranges and sills.
What do these range values imply for our strategy of sampling independent points? They are all several times larger than the typical scale of a landslide initiation zone. Even if we use the ~300-m range found for gradient as the minimum point spacing, that precludes more than one sample point per landslide initiation zone. Ultimately the goal is to have a representative sample that accurately reflects the range and frequency distribution of predictor values across both the observed initiation zones and the masked area spanning the range of values observed within the initiation zones. Independence precludes bias in our representation of those frequency distributions, but bias will still be an issue if we have too few points.



How many randomly placed sample points do we need to obtain an accurate and complete representation of the frequency distribution of predictor values? That depends on how uniform the frequency distributions are. Long-tailed distributions require more points to ensure that rare conditions get samples. We can compare frequency distributions of sample-point sets to those of the predictor rasters. This is straight forward for single predictors, but becomes more complicated when looking at combinations of predictors.
Representative sample, curse of dimensionality.


Let’s start with one sample point per initiation zone and a point spacing of 300m elsewhere. We can generate point samples with program samplePoints.

I found building cumulative distributions with R to be rather slow, so program samplePoints also outputs a csv file with a histogram for each of the predictors. We can use these to compare the frequency distributions of the sample points to those of the predictor rasters. The files have five columns:

Lower, the lower bound of the interval
Upper, the upper bound of the interval
Mid, the midpoint of the interval
Number, the number of DEM cells or sample points included in the interval
CumulativeProp, the cumulative proportion of cells or samples

There are three things of interest, examined in the follows plots:

How different, or similar, are the frequency distributions for areas inside and outside of the initiation zones? This difference is characterized using the landslide density as a function of the predictor value, here calculated in terms of initiation-zone-area/total-area (limited only to the masked region). Density is calculated for each interval used to build the histograms.
How well do the frequency distributions for the sample points match those of the area sampled?
How well does the variation in landslide density indicated by the sample match the actual landslide density measured using all DEM grid points?

  pbar_in1[[i]] <- ggplot() +

    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),

  pbar_out1[[i]] <- ggplot() +

    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),

    theme(legend.title = element_blank(),

In these examples, the initiation zones have one third as many points as the total analysis area, but they also cover only 1/1000th of the total analysis area, so the number of points per unit area in the initiation zones is about 300 times higher.
I will use the sample points from the third example to train and validate a machine-learning model to predict landslide initiation probability. I’m using the R package mlr3, which simplifies implementation of a machine-learning workflow.
